summary: Gruber argues that full-text RSS feeds are not the revenue killers that traditional publishers fear, using Daring Fireball's own experience as evidence. He responds to Jason Snell's skepticism about whether full-text feeds hurt web traffic by showing that while his page views initially stagnated after switching to full-content RSS, readership grew dramatically through feed subscribers, and page views eventually doubled. He contends that the real problem is a business model fixated on page views as a proxy for attention, when in fact RSS subscribers represent the most attentive readers. Gruber frames attention — not page views — as the true commodity publishers sell to advertisers.
key_points: Daring Fireball's full-content RSS feed became the site's single largest revenue source, contradicting the assumption that RSS ads are inherently cheap and ineffective
After switching to full-content RSS, page views stagnated for a year but readership via feed subscribers skyrocketed, and page views eventually doubled
Web page views are a poor proxy for reader attention — RSS subscribers pay the most attention but generate the fewest page views
The problem isn't full-content RSS feeds but business models that hinge solely on page views as a revenue metric
A reader requesting full-content RSS is actively seeking to pay more attention, which publishers should find ways to monetize
money_quotes: The precious commodity that we, as publishers, have to offer advertisers is the attention of our readers.
Web page views are a terribly inaccurate, if not outright misleading, metric for attention.
Subscribers to a full-content RSS feed are among the readers paying the most attention, but generate among the least web page views.
A reader asking for a full-content RSS feed is a reader who wants to pay more attention to what you publish. There have to be ways to thrive financially from that.
The problem, I say, isn't with full-content RSS feeds, but rather with a business model that hinges solely on web page views.
The ads in most sponsored RSS feeds are indeed cheap and lousy. The ads in DF's RSS feed are neither.
key_insight: Attention, not page views, is the real commodity publishers sell — and full-content RSS feeds attract the most attentive readers, making them an asset rather than a threat to revenue.
